q: Trace a piece of undigested food from the mouth to the anus.
a: Oral cavity (mouth/pharynx) -> esophagus -> stomach -> small intestine (duodenum, jejunum, ileum) -> large intestine (colon, rectum) -> anus.
The long muscular tube from the stomach to the anus consisting of the esophagus, stomach, small intestine, and large intestine.
The portion of the gastrointestinal tract running specifically from the stomach to the anus.
The soupy mixture of food and secretions created in the gut.
The mouth and pharynx; serves as a receptacle for food and where the first stages of chemical and mechanical digestion begin.
Accessory exocrine glands (sublingual, submandibular, parotid) that secrete saliva into the mouth to begin digestion.
A narrow tube of skeletal and smooth muscle that travels through the thorax to move swallowed food to the stomach.
A baglike organ (fundus, body, antrum) that stores food and mixes it with acid and enzymes to create chyme.
An accessory glandular organ that secretes digestive enzymes and bicarbonate (exocrine) into the duodenum, and insulin/glucagon (endocrine).
An accessory organ that secretes bile for fat digestion and filters absorbed nutrients arriving via the hepatic portal system.
The segment of the GI tract where most digestion and nearly all absorption occurs; consists of the duodenum, jejunum, and ileum.
The first 25 cm of the small intestine where chyme enters and is mixed with pancreatic and liver secretions.
The middle section of the small intestine, heavily involved in the digestion and absorption of nutrients.
The terminal section of the small intestine that absorbs remaining nutrients (like Vitamin B12 and bile salts) before emptying into the colon.
The final section of the GI tract (colon and rectum) responsible for absorbing water and electrolytes to form solid feces.
The proximal section of the large intestine where watery chyme becomes semisolid feces via water absorption.
The semisolid waste remaining in the lumen at the end of the GI tract after water and electrolytes have been absorbed.
The terminal section of the large intestine where distension by feces triggers the defecation reflex.
The opening at the end of the digestive tract, guarded by an external anal sphincter of skeletal muscle under voluntary control.

q: Describe the layers of the GI tract wall and how they vary in composition throughout the digestive system to achieve different functions.
a: 1) Mucosa: inner secretory/absorptive lining (epithelium, lamina propria, muscularis mucosae). 2) Submucosa: connective tissue with vessels/nerves. 3) Muscularis externa: circular/longitudinal smooth muscle for motility. 4) Serosa: outer connective covering.
Small fingerlike extensions of the intestinal mucosa that project into the lumen to increase the surface area for absorption.
Tubular invaginations of the surface epithelium extending down into the connective tissue of the stomach; site of acid and enzyme secretion.
Tubular invaginations in the intestine where fluid/hormone secretion and stem cell renewal occur.
Deepest invaginations of the mucosa that form secretory glands opening into the gut lumen through ducts.
The inner lining of the GI tract containing a single layer of mucosal epithelium, the lamina propria, and the muscularis mucosae.
The middle layer of the gut wall composed of connective tissue, containing larger blood/lymph vessels and the submucosal nerve plexus.
The outer wall of the GI tract consisting of an inner circular layer and an outer longitudinal layer of smooth muscle for gut motility.
The outer covering of the entire digestive tract composed of a connective tissue membrane (a continuation of the peritoneum).
The transporting/secretory cell layer facing the lumen. The apical (mucosal) surface faces the lumen; the basolateral (serosal) surface faces the ECF to transfer absorbed materials.
Subepithelial connective tissue containing nerve fibers, small blood/lymph vessels, and wandering immune cells of the GALT.
A thin layer of smooth muscle separating the lamina propria from the submucosa; its contraction waves villi to alter the effective absorptive surface area.

q: Describe the primary function of the digestive system.
a: To move nutrients, water, and electrolytes from the external environment into the body's internal environment.
The movement of water and ions from the ECF to the digestive tract lumen, OR the release of substances synthesized by GI epithelial cells into the lumen or ECF.
The chemical and mechanical breakdown of foods into smaller units that can be taken across the intestinal epithelium into the body.
The movement of substances (nutrients, water, ions) from the lumen of the GI tract to the extracellular fluid (ECF).
The movement of material in the GI tract as a result of muscle contraction.

q: Locate where secretion occurs in the GI tract.
a: Occurs via exocrine glands (salivary glands, pancreas, liver) and epithelial cells throughout the stomach and small intestine.

q: Locate where digestion occurs in the GI tract.
a: Mechanical and chemical breakdown begins in the mouth, continues heavily in the stomach, and is completed in the small intestine.

q: Locate where absorption occurs in the GI tract.
a: Most takes place in the small intestine, with additional absorption of water and ions occurring in the large intestine.

q: Locate where motility occurs in the GI tract.
a: Occurs continuously from the mouth (swallowing) through the esophagus, stomach, small intestine, and large intestine.

q: Discuss the role of mass balance within the digestive system.
a: The body secretes roughly 7L of fluid daily into the GI tract, plus 2L ingested. To maintain mass balance and prevent dehydration, nearly all 9L must be absorbed, leaving only ~0.1L excreted in feces.

q: Explain the challenge of autodigestion that our digestive system faces, and how our bodies overcome this challenge.
a: Powerful digestive enzymes could destroy GI cells. The body overcomes this by secreting enzymes as inactive zymogens and protecting the epithelium with a mucus-bicarbonate barrier.

q: Explain the challenge of mass balance that our digestive system faces, and how our bodies overcome this challenge.
a: Massive fluid volume is secreted daily (1/6th of total body water). The body overcomes this by highly efficient water and ion reabsorption in the small and large intestines.

q: Explain the challenge of defense that our digestive system faces, and how our bodies overcome this challenge.
a: The massive surface area is vulnerable to ingested pathogens. Overcome via physiological defense mechanisms: mucus, digestive enzymes, acidic pH, and the gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT).

q: Discuss the roles of digestive enzymes within the process of digestion.
a: Secreted by exocrine glands or epithelial cells, they chemically break down macromolecules into absorbable units. They function best at specific pH levels (e.g., stomach enzymes at acidic pH).

q: Discuss the roles of mucus within the process of digestion.
a: A viscous secretion of glycoproteins (mucins) that forms a protective coating over the GI mucosa and lubricates the contents of the gut.
Progressive waves of contraction that move from one section to the next, responsible for the forward movement of a food bolus.
Short segments of intestine alternately contract and relax. This churns and mixes the contents to maximize contact with the absorptive epithelium, with little net forward movement.

q: Describe and compare the migrating motor complex.
a: A 'housekeeping' series of contractions that occurs between meals, sweeping slowly from the stomach to the large intestine to clear out food remnants and bacteria.

q: Describe the intestinal phase of digestion.
a: Begins when chyme enters the small intestine. Controlled by neural and endocrine reflexes, it involves slowing gastric emptying, neutralizing acid with bicarbonate, enzymatic digestion, and maximal nutrient absorption.

q: Explain how the anatomy of the small intestine facilitates secretion, digestion, and absorption.
a: It maximizes surface area with macroscopic plicae and villi, and microscopic enterocyte microvilli (brush border). Crypts handle fluid secretion, while villi contain capillaries and lacteals for massive absorption.

q: Understand the role of the pancreas in endocrine functions.
a: Pancreatic islet cells secrete hormones (insulin and glucagon) directly into the blood to regulate metabolism.

q: Understand the role of the pancreas in exocrine functions.
a: Pancreatic acinar cells secrete digestive enzymes (as zymogens), and duct cells secrete a watery sodium bicarbonate solution into the duodenum to neutralize acid and digest food.

q: Describe the role of Digestive enzymes in digestion and where they are made/secreted.
a: Made/secreted by the exocrine pancreas (acinar cells) and small intestine (brush border). They complete the chemical breakdown of polypeptides, carbohydrates, and fats.

q: Describe the role of Bile and bile salts in digestion and where they are made/secreted.
a: Made by liver hepatocytes and secreted/stored in the gallbladder. They act as amphipathic detergents to emulsify coarse fat droplets, increasing surface area for enzymatic digestion.

q: Describe the role of Bicarbonate in digestion and where it is made/secreted.
a: Made/secreted primarily by pancreatic duct cells (and some duodenal cells) into the small intestine to neutralize the highly acidic chyme entering from the stomach.

q: Describe the role of Mucus in digestion and where it is made/secreted.
a: Made/secreted by goblet cells in the intestine (and mucous cells in the stomach/salivary glands). It acts as a physical barrier to protect the epithelium and lubricates gut contents.

q: Describe the role of Isotonic NaCl solution (CFTR channels) in digestion and where it is made/secreted.
a: Made/secreted by crypt cells in the small intestine and colon. It mixes with mucus to help lubricate gut contents; driven by Cl- leaving via the apical CFTR channel.

q: Explain and diagram the digestion of fats, and where this takes place in the digestive system.
a: In the small intestine: Bile salts emulsify large fat droplets. Pancreatic lipase (aided by colipase) digests triglycerides into monoglycerides and free fatty acids, forming micelles.

q: Explain and diagram the absorption of fats, and where this takes place in the digestive system.
a: In the small intestine: Lipophilic fatty acids/monoglycerides diffuse into enterocytes. They recombine into triglycerides in the smooth ER, join cholesterol/proteins to form chylomicrons, exocytose, and enter lacteals.
Having both a hydrophobic region and a hydrophilic region. Example: Bile salts, which use this property to coat lipid droplets and make them water-soluble emulsions.
Enzymes (e.g., from the pancreas) that carry out enzymatic fat digestion by removing two fatty acids from each triglyceride molecule.
A protein cofactor secreted by the pancreas that displaces some bile salts on an emulsion droplet, allowing lipase access to the fats inside.
Small disk-shaped particles formed in the intestinal lumen from fatty acids, bile salts, mono/diglycerides, and cholesterol, which ferry lipids to the brush border for absorption.
A pancreatic enzyme that specifically digests phospholipids in the small intestine.
Large droplets formed inside enterocytes from recombined triglycerides, cholesterol, and proteins. They are exocytosed and enter the lymphatic system.
Lymph vessels of the intestinal villi that absorb chylomicrons (which are too large to cross capillary basement membranes) and transport them to the venous circulation.

q: Explain and diagram the digestion of carbohydrates, and where this takes place in the digestive system.
a: Begins in mouth (salivary amylase), resumes in small intestine (pancreatic amylase digesting starch to maltose). Intestinal brush-border enzymes (disaccharidases) then digest disaccharides into absorbable monosaccharides.

q: Explain and diagram the absorption of carbohydrates, and where this takes place in the digestive system.
a: In the small intestine: Glucose/galactose enter via apical Na+-SGLT symporter. Fructose enters via apical GLUT5. All exit the basolateral membrane via GLUT2 facilitated diffusion.
Complex glucose polymers. Starch and glycogen are digestible by amylase. Cellulose cannot be digested by humans and acts as dietary fiber.
Two-sugar molecules that must be broken down by brush-border disaccharidases before they can be absorbed.
Single-sugar molecules that are the absorbable end products of carbohydrate digestion.
Enzyme secreted by salivary glands and pancreas that breaks long glucose polymers (starch) into smaller chains and maltose.
Indigestible plant carbohydrates (like cellulose) that lack human digestive enzymes; excreted as roughage.
An intestinal brush border enzyme that digests the disaccharide lactose (milk sugar) into glucose and galactose.
Apical Na+-dependent symporter on enterocytes that moves glucose and galactose from the lumen into the cell.
Basolateral transporter that moves glucose, galactose, and fructose out of the enterocyte into the ECF.
Apical transporter that moves fructose into the enterocyte via Na+-independent facilitated diffusion.

q: Explain and diagram the digestion of proteins, and where this takes place in the digestive system.
a: Begins in stomach (pepsin activated by acid). Completes in small intestine via pancreatic endopeptidases (trypsin, chymotrypsin) and exopeptidases (carboxypeptidases), breaking proteins into amino acids/peptides.

q: Explain and diagram the absorption of proteins, and where this takes place in the digestive system.
a: In the small intestine: Free amino acids use Na+-dependent cotransport. Di/tripeptides enter via H+-dependent PepT1 (mostly digested intracellularly). Larger peptides are absorbed via transcytosis.
Also known as proteases. Enzymes that attack internal peptide bonds of an amino acid chain, breaking it into smaller fragments (e.g., pepsin, trypsin).
Synonym for endopeptidases. Secreted as inactive zymogens from the stomach, intestine, and pancreas to prevent autodigestion.
A stomach endopeptidase secreted as inactive pepsinogen by chief cells and activated by H+ (acid); begins protein digestion.
A pancreatic endopeptidase secreted as trypsinogen and activated by brush border enteropeptidase; it digests proteins and activates other pancreatic zymogens.
Enzymes that release single amino acids from peptides by chopping them off the ends one at a time (e.g., aminopeptidase, carboxypeptidase).

q: Explain and diagram the cellular mechanisms for the secretion or absorption of water.
a: Water follows osmotic gradients created by solute absorption (e.g., Na+). It moves mostly via the passive, paracellular pathway in the small intestine and colon.

q: Explain and diagram the cellular mechanisms for the secretion or absorption of vitamins.
a: Fat-soluble vitamins (A,D,E,K) absorb with fats. Water-soluble (B,C) use mediated transport. B12 requires binding to intrinsic factor to be absorbed in the ileum.

q: Explain and diagram the cellular mechanisms for the secretion or absorption of minerals/ions.
a: Na+ absorbs via apical channels/symporters. Iron absorbs actively (DMT1). Calcium absorbs passively paracellularly or via regulated transepithelial transport (vitamin D3). Cl- secretion drives fluid secretion via CFTR.

q: Keyword: fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)
a: Vitamins that are lipophilic and absorbed in the small intestine along with dietary fats/micelles.
Vitamins absorbed by mediated transport mechanisms across the intestinal epithelium.
A water-soluble vitamin that is exclusively absorbed in the ileum only when complexed with intrinsic factor.
A protein secreted by gastric parietal cells that binds to Vitamin B12 to allow its intestinal absorption.
Mineral absorbed as heme or ionized Fe2+ via active transport (DMT1). Its uptake is regulated by the liver hormone hepcidin.
Mineral whose regulated transepithelial absorption in the duodenum is controlled by vitamin D3; it also absorbs passively via paracellular routes.

q: Explain the neural control of the intestinal phase of digestion.
a: Chyme entering the intestine activates the enteric nervous system (ENS), which initiates short reflexes to decrease gastric motility, slow gastric emptying, and promote intestinal motility/secretion.

q: Explain the hormonal control of the intestinal phase of digestion.
a: Secretin (triggered by acid), CCK (fats), and GIP/GLP-1 (carbs) feed back to slow gastric emptying and acid secretion, while promoting pancreatic bicarbonate/enzyme release and insulin secretion.
The 'little brain' nerve plexuses in the gut wall that integrate short reflexes for independent control of motility and secretion.
Hormone released by acidic chyme in the duodenum; it stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion and inhibits gastric emptying and acid secretion.
Hormone released by fats/amino acids; it stimulates gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme secretion, while slowing gastric emptying.
Incretin hormone released by carbs/fats; feeds forward to stimulate insulin release from the pancreas and inhibits gastric function.
Incretin hormone released by mixed meals; stimulates insulin release, inhibits glucagon, slows gastric function, and promotes satiety.
END
